title: How to Read a Book You Totally Hate for English Class
author: Tklesson Team
content:
You've been assigned The Scarlet Letter. Or Moby Dick. Or something by Jane Austen. You have zero interest. The language is old. The sentences are long. You'd rather do almost anything else. Here is how to get the A anyway without pulling your hair out.

Don't Read It Like a Modern Book

Classics weren't written for teens in 2024. They were written for adults in another century. Reading every word is a trap. Instead, focus on the main action and skip the long descriptions. Did the author spend a page describing wallpaper? Skip it. Is that important to the plot? Almost never.

This is called 'selective reading.' You're not being graded on whether you read every word. You're being graded on whether you understand the themes, characters, and major events.

Use SparkNotes or LitCharts Strategically

Teachers say not to use SparkNotes. What they mean is: don't use SparkNotes instead of reading. But using it alongside reading is smart. Read a chapter summary first. Then read the actual chapter. You'll already know what to look for, so the old language won't confuse you as much.

This is how many adult readers get through classics. There's no shame in using a map before you hike the trail.

Listen to the Audiobook While Following Along

Hearing the words while seeing them helps your brain process old sentence structures. Most classic novels are free on YouTube or LibriVox. Speed it up to 1.25x or 1.5x. Suddenly, it sounds like a normal person talking instead of a bored professor lecturing.

Also: audiobooks make commutes, chores, and workouts into reading time. You're not losing hours of your life — you're multitasking.

Focus on Character Lists and Plot Points

If you remember three things from each chapter, you're fine. Who did what? Why did they do it? What changed? Everything else is decoration. Write down these three things as you go. By the end of the book, you'll have a complete summary.

Most English teachers will tell you exactly what they want you to notice in class discussions. Pay attention to those clues. If the teacher keeps talking about symbolism, that's what will be on the test.

Read the First and Last Sentence of Each Paragraph

This is a speed-reading trick. The first sentence introduces the idea. The last sentence concludes it. The middle sentences are examples and details. If you understand the main idea, you can often skip the examples.

Try it for one chapter. You'll be surprised how much you still understand.

Watch the Movie Adaptation (But Don't Stop There)

Movies change things. But they give you the general plot and characters. Watch the movie first. Now you have a mental framework. Then read the book. You'll spend less time figuring out who is who and more time noticing what the movie left out — which is exactly what your teacher will ask about on the test.

The goal is not to love the book. The goal is to pass the class. Use every tool available. Get the grade. Move on with your life.
